title: podman + sam locally stuck on "Testing application behaviour against authorizers..." step
body:
### Issue Description

Hello,

I have been trying to set up podman and sam to run a serverless app backend locally. When I use docker it works normally and my lambda functions mount, however when running podman sam gets stuck on this "Testing application behaviour..."step without proceeding to mount my functions. The same issue was observed by other members of our team.

My device: Apple M1 Pro
sam version: SAM CLI, version 1.132.0
podman: podman version 5.3.1
Docker cli: Docker version 27.3.1, build ce1223035a
Docker-compose: Docker Compose version 2.31.0

For context,
My aws config is set up correctly. 
Podman is running and my containers are mapping to the right ports, I am able to ping them with curl.
I have also tried setting my DOCKER_HOST path as [here](https://podman-desktop.io/docs/migrating-from-docker/using-the-docker_host-environment-variable#:~:text=docker%20context%20create%20podman%20%2D%2Ddocker%20%22host%3Dunix%3A//%24HOME.local/share/containers/podman/machine/podman.sock%22)
```bash
docker context create podman --docker "host=unix://$HOME.local/share/containers/podman/machine/podman.sock"
```
and then running my api after sam build has been successful:
```bash
SAM_CLI_CONTAINER_RUNTIME=podman sam local start-api --env-vars .env.json --parameter-overrides EnvironmentClientIdKey=local
```
However, sam remains hanging indefinitely not mounting functions
```bash
sam local start-api --env-vars .env.json --parameter-overrides EnvironmentClientIdKey=local 
No current session found, using default AWS::AccountId                                                 
                                                                                                       
AWS SAM CLI does not guarantee 100% fidelity between authorizers locally                               
and authorizers deployed on AWS. Any application critical behavior should                              
be validated thoroughly before deploying to production.                                                
                                                                                                       
Testing application behaviour against authorizers deployed on AWS can be done using the sam sync       
command.                                                                                               
                                                                                                       
```

Running simply ```sam local start-api ``` or ```sam local start-api --env-vars .env.json --parameter-overrides EnvironmentClientIdKey=local``` also gets stuck without mounting lambda functions. 

I originally reported it to the sam repo [here](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/issues/7798) and then found another person reporting the issue on the sam repo [here](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/issues/7298#issuecomment-2542083461) and a workaround [here](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/issues/7298#issuecomment-2308885975)

# **_However, since sam works seamlessly with docker, I wonder if there is anything that could be changed on podman to avoid this issue. Thank you._**  

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. sam build
2. podman compose up
3. sam local start-api --env-vars .env.json


### Describe the results you received

sam hangs without mounting functions 
```bash
sam local start-api --env-vars .env.json --parameter-overrides EnvironmentClientIdKey=local 
No current session found, using default AWS::AccountId                                                 
                                                                                                       
AWS SAM CLI does not guarantee 100% fidelity between authorizers locally                               
and authorizers deployed on AWS. Any application critical behavior should                              
be validated thoroughly before deploying to production.                                                
                                                                                                       
Testing application behaviour against authorizers deployed on AWS can be done using the sam sync       
command.                                                                                               
                                                                                                       
```

### Describe the results you expected

I would expect functions to mount and to be able to ping them

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

comments.body: does it work rootfully with podman?
@baude no it does not work, I am admin on my machine

@baude Since I am admin on my machine I assume it runs as root?
Also tried using the explicit flag suggested by copilot
```bash
podman machine start --privileged
Error: unknown flag: --privileged
See 'podman machine start --help'
```
but got an error.
I tried this too
```bash
podman compose up --privileged
>>>> Executing external compose provider "/opt/homebrew/bin/docker-compose". Please see podman-compose(1) for how to disable this message. <<<<

unknown flag: --privileged
Error: executing /opt/homebrew/bin/docker-compose up --privileged: exit status 16
```

Not knowing what the AWS CLI is doing exactly, it's hard to say what's going wrong. Switching the machine to rootful with `podman machine set --rootful` as @baude suggested might help. If that doesn't work, you could try pulling trace-level logs by SSHing into the machine VM, stopping the `podman.service` API, and running `podman system service -t 0 --log-level=trace` to see if anything pops out in the logs, but that's going to be looking for a needle in a haystack - we really need to know what exactly the SAM tool is doing that fails to debug effectively.

@baude @mheon Thank you for your suggestions I have tried them out but the same issue remains:
```bash
podman machine set --rootful
podman machine start        
Starting machine "podman-machine-default"
API forwarding listening on: /var/run/docker.sock
Docker API clients default to this address. You do not need to set DOCKER_HOST.

Machine "podman-machine-default" started successfully
podman compose up           
>>>> Executing external compose provider "/opt/homebrew/bin/docker-compose". Please see podman-compose(1) for how to disable this message. <<<<

[+] Running 14/14
................
...............

w Enable Watch
```

Then when running sam it remains hanging.
Is there a reason why this [workaround solution](https://github.com/aws/aws-sam-cli/issues/7298#issuecomment-2308885975) solves it and why sam works seamlessly when using docker?
Oh, that helps a lot. Looks like a difference in how we handle events based on the pull request.

Can you provide the the full `podman info` output, is 5.3.1 only the client version or does it actually use the same version on the server.
I already fixed an issue that was blocking the event response until an event was generated in 5.3.0: https://github.com/containers/podman/commit/768ad8653af4cebd069a865a5dbf4a7ec8517444

@Luap99 Is there anything unusual? I have Podman Desktop 1.14.2 release and ```podman --version

>   Version: 5.2.5

Your server version is is not updated automatically. You have to either remove and init the machine again if you have any data in there that you do not want to loose you can use
`podman machine os apply --restart quay.io/podman/machine-os:5.3`

@Luap99 Thank you! This actually fixed the issue and I was able to successfully run sam seamlessly!
Here is what I did:
```bash
podman machine rm
podman machine init
podman machine os apply --restart quay.io/podman/machine-os:5.3
podman machine start
podman info // confirmed that last line is now Version: 5.3
podman compose up
sam local start-api --env-vars .env.json --parameter-overrides EnvironmentClientIdKey=local // in another terminal
```
